Four years later, it's expanded to ban other companies from selling such data to brokers, and to specifically cover  …

title: Google’s NotebookLM can sum up your research in a TikTok-style clip

title: Meet the lawyer who beat Elon Musk — twice
content:
“After college, my plan was to come to New York and get a record deal.” | Cath Virginia / The Verge, Getty Images	

Watching Elon Musk fulminate at Bill Savitt during Musk v. Altman - the case in which Musk sued Sam Altman and OpenAI instead of seeing a therapist about his AI failures - was a bit like watching a toddler have a temper tantrum at his nursery school teacher. Savitt's questions were "designed to trick me," Musk said. He also told Savitt at one point, "You mostly do unfair questions."
Savitt, who has the approximate demeanor of a handsome Droopy Dog, gently told Musk, "I am trying to put the questions as fairly as I can. I am doing my best." 
I've seen a number of styles of cross-examination. Savitt's was mild-mannered and soft-spoken; his q …

summary: A lawyer named Bill Savitt has successfully represented clients against Elon Musk in two cases, including a recent one where Musk sued OpenAI and its CEO Sam Altman. Musk became frustrated during the cross-examination, accusing Savitt of asking unfair questions. Savitt remained calm and professional throughout the exchange.

title: Why California’s carbon manure math doesn’t add up
content: Something stinks in California’s climate policies. Years ago, the state set up a system that pays cattle farmers across the country to turn the methane emitted from cattle manure into natural gas, encouraging the dairy sector to produce a gas we burn instead of one that just pollutes the air. It’s become wildly popular because…
summary: California's climate policy pays cattle farmers to turn methane from cattle manure into natural gas, but the math behind this system is being questioned. However, the effectiveness and accuracy of this system are now under scrutiny.
